title: Interest rates: Two rate rises ahead as Iran shock spreads
full_text: Other Editions 04 May 2026 Market Wrap Interest rates: Two rate rises ahead as Iran shock spreads The Australian Financial Review Cecile Lefort 30 April 2026 703 words English AFNR 21 Copyright 2026. Fairfax Media Management Pty Limited. Financial markets are bracing for at least two more interest rate increases from the Reserve Bank of Australia this year, which would push borrowing costs to the highest since 2011 as the inflationary shockwave from the conflict in Iran intensifies even as the economy slows. The central bank faces a delicate balancing act when the board meets next week after data on Wednesday showed core inflation for the first quarter, the RBA's preferred gauge, hit 3.5 per cent in the year to March. While other figures in the report were not as bad as some had feared, they also remain well above the RBA's 2.5 per cent target. Barrenjoey chief rates strategist Andrew Lilley noted that while core inflation, which excludes fuel, was running about 20 basis points lower than where the RBA expected in February, it would not be enough to stop a third rate rise from the bank this year. "That's going to give them some comfort, but not enough to hold the cash rate unchanged next week," he said. The pressure stems largely from the Middle East. Oil prices have soared nearly 60 per cent to above $US110 a barrel since the Iran war erupted in late February, all but closing the Strait of Hormuz, a vital shipping route for about one-fifth of the world's oil supplies. Because Australia imports 90 per cent of its refined fuel, and petrol makes up 3.3 per cent of the CPI, the impact on households in March was sharp. Energy costs are expected to bleed into the price of other goods and services, keeping inflation high for months. "The RBA still has a coming concern: what will higher fuel prices do to the rest of the CPI basket?" Lilley said. The RBA has already lifted the cash rate twice this year to combat price growth. Following Wednesday's inflation data, bond traders modestly trimmed the chance of a rate rise on May 5 to 4.35 per cent to 75 per cent, down from 88 per cent. However, traders are sticking to their bet of two increases before Christmas. Markets are fully priced for a move in June and again in September, which would take the cash rate to 4.7 per cent, the highest since 2011. Adding to the challenge is a tight Australian labour market and an economy running at full capacity. "Inflation jumped nearly a full percentage point from the previous month, pushing the annual rate to its highest level in 30 months, a very clear hint of what is to come," said Krishna Bhimavarapu, APAC economist at State Street Investment Management. He argued that the RBA should act now to avoid long-term damage. "While the data can still be read as allowing a hold, we think the RBA should hike and drive the economy into a slow lane now, rather than risk more durable inflation damage later." Prashant Newnaha, rate strategist at TD Securities, agreed that high crude prices and an economy running at full speed supported the case for another rate rise, but said the "cash rate outlook beyond that is less clear". The debate is not one-sided. Some investors believe the board might find enough evidence to pause in May, especially as petrol prices have retreated from their peaks lately. Renny Ellis, head of portfolio management at Arculus Funds Management, suggested a pause would give the board time to assess the impact of the Strait of Hormuz blockade and the federal budget due out on May 12. But for Christian Baylis, founder of Fortlake Asset Management, there was no doubt that energy costs would continue to filter through the economy. He argued the central bank must be proactive. "The path of least resistance is to hike, get on top of the problem, and then if you think you've gone too far, you can easily reverse course and bring it back," he said. "You are better off front-running the inflation issue, ensuring the upside and then delivering more cuts and solving the problem that way, as opposed to allowing it to become entrenched." summary: Financial markets anticipate at least two more interest rate hikes from the Reserve Bank of Australia (RBA) this year, driven by rising inflation linked to the conflict in Iran. Core inflation has reached 3.5%, exceeding the RBA's target, prompting concerns about the impact of escalating energy costs on the broader economy. Analysts suggest that the RBA may need to act preemptively to mitigate long-term inflation risks.
publication:
Chalmers talks a big game - it's time to act on intergenerational fairness
The Age02 May 20261,330 wordsMillie Muroi

When we debate intergenerational fairness, it often looks like younger Australians lamenting the selfishness of Boomers and those Boomers calling Zoomers "entitled" for wanting more.
heading: Banking and Economy
key_points: Interest rates are expected to rise to the highest levels since 2011 due to inflationary pressures from the Iran conflict.
Core inflation is currently at 3.5%, above the RBA's target of 2.5%.
Market expectations include two rate increases before the end of the year.
relevance: Understanding interest rate trends and inflation is crucial for actuaries and financial services professionals as it impacts investment strategies and risk assessments.
